worth_it_explanation: US state privacy obligations attach to the publisher who runs Prebid.js, and the opt-out signal a visitor sets only reaches the bidders if the page is configured to read it and act on it. A missing or misconfigured GPP control module means an opt-out a visitor expressed is collected by the consent management platform but never enforced inside the auction, which is a legal exposure rather than a revenue one. The check reads the Prebid configuration the publisher already runs in the browser, so it costs inspection time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a specific module and config block the publisher can correct.
intro: Prebid.js is the header-bidding library a publisher runs on their own pages, so the publisher carries the obligation to honor US state privacy choices that visitors make. The Global Privacy Platform (GPP) is the IAB Tech Lab container that encodes those choices, and Prebid reads it through the consent management Global Privacy Platform module and acts on it through the GPP control modules. This subcategory checks whether the opt-out a visitor sets actually reaches the demand running through Prebid, and whether the signal the page sends matches the jurisdiction logic the publisher applies. These checks read the Prebid configuration in the browser rather than rebuilding anything, so they confirm the privacy plumbing is wired end to end.

audit_checks.title: GPP control module is not configured to enforce US opt-out signals
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When a visitor opts out of sale or sharing but the GPP control module is absent or inactive, the opt-out is recorded by the consent management platform yet never enforced inside the Prebid auction, so the bidders receive the request as if no opt-out occurred. That is a direct gap between the choice a visitor expressed and what the page does, which is the legal exposure US state privacy laws are written to prevent.

educate.base:
Prebid.js handles US state privacy in two layers, and both have to be present for an opt-out to take effect. The first layer is the consent management Global Privacy Platform (GPP) module, which reads the GPP string from an IAB-compliant consent management platform (CMP) and makes the visitor's privacy choices available to the auction. The second layer is a GPP control module, which reads those choices and applies the activity restrictions that suppress privacy-sensitive behavior such as sharing user-provided first-party data with bidders. The consent management module alone collects the signal. The control module is what acts on it.

For US national coverage, the 'gppControl_usnat' module reads section 7 of the GPP string, the USNat string, and applies conservative activity controls based on what the visitor opted out of. Prebid activates this module automatically when the 'gpp' object exists in the consent management configuration and the consent management GPP module is loaded. One detail matters here, the USNat control module works with dynamic GPP data delivered through the CMP and the 'setConfig()' call, and it does not act on a static section 7 string placed in the first-party data 'regs.gpp' object. A publisher who supplies the string statically without the control module sees the string present but the restrictions never applied.

The activity controls are the mechanism that connects the opt-out to the auction. When the USNat string encodes a sale opt-out or a targeted-advertising opt-out, the control module restricts activities such as 'transmitUfpd', so the user-provided first-party data that would otherwise reach bidders is held back. Without the control module, those activities run unrestricted regardless of what the visitor chose.

A few signs point to the GPP control module being absent or inactive:
  • The Prebid build does not include 'consentManagementGpp' alongside a control module such as 'gppControl_usnat'
  • The consent management configuration has no 'gpp' object, so the control module never activates
  • The GPP string is supplied statically through 'regs.gpp' with no control module loaded, so the string is present but no restriction is applied
  • Bid requests carry user-provided first-party data even when the GPP string encodes a sale or sharing opt-out
You may want to confirm that a GPP control module is loaded and active, not just that the consent management GPP module is collecting the string. One approach is to set an opt-out in the CMP, then inspect the resulting bid requests to see whether the restricted data is actually withheld.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, much of the inventory runs programmatically through header bidding, so a missing GPP control module exposes the bulk of monetized impressions to an unenforced opt-out. You may want to verify the control module against the placements that carry the most programmatic demand since those are where an unenforced opt-out reaches the most bidders.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, audience data tied to shopping intent is the data most likely to be shared with bidders, which is exactly the data a sale or sharing opt-out is meant to restrict. You may want to confirm the control module withholds user-provided first-party data on the commercial pages, where the data at stake carries the most value to demand.

learn_more.source_title: Consent Management - GPP for Header Bidding
Prebid US Compliance Support for Header Bidding

investigate.intro: Confirm whether a GPP control module is loaded and active, then check that an opt-out set in the consent management platform actually restricts the data reaching bidders.

browser_console.label: Browser console and Prebid debug
steps: Open the page in the browser, open developer tools, and switch to the Console tab.
Run 'pbjs.installedModules' and confirm the list includes 'consentManagementGpp' and a control module such as 'gppControl_usnat' or 'gppControl_usstates'.
Run 'pbjs.getConfig('consentManagement')' and confirm a 'gpp' object exists with a 'cmpApi' of 'iab' for a live CMP because the control module activates only when the 'gpp' object is present.
Run 'pbjs.getEvents()' after an auction and look for the GPP string and consent data being read into the auction.
Set an opt-out for sale or sharing in the CMP, reload, and inspect a sample bid request in the Network tab to confirm the user-provided first-party data is withheld rather than sent.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether a GPP control module is loaded alongside the consent management GPP module, whether the consent management configuration includes a 'gpp' object so the control module activates, and whether an opt-out set in the CMP withholds the restricted data in the resulting bid requests. A setup where a control module is present, active, and visibly restricts data on an opt-out passes. A setup where the control module is missing, where no 'gpp' object activates it, or where bid requests still carry user-provided first-party data after an opt-out is a fail recorded with the module and config gap found.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether a GPP control module is loaded, whether the 'gpp' config object activates it, and whether an opt-out withholds the restricted data above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} reads US privacy choices through the consent management Global Privacy Platform (GPP) module but does not load a GPP control module to enforce them, so an opt-out a visitor sets is collected yet never applied inside the Prebid auction. The consent management module alone makes the GPP string available, while a control module such as 'gppControl_usnat' is what restricts activities like sharing user-provided first-party data when the string encodes a sale or sharing opt-out. {{voice_recommend}} adding the appropriate GPP control module to the Prebid build, confirming the consent management configuration includes a 'gpp' object so the module activates, and testing that an opt-out set in the consent management platform visibly withholds the restricted data in the resulting bid requests.

audit_checks.title: The GPP signal does not match the jurisdiction logic the site applies
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Prebid acts on whatever GPP string the consent management platform supplies, so if the platform sends a national string where the publisher intended state-level handling, or applies the wrong sections for a visitor's state, Prebid faithfully enforces a signal that does not reflect the publisher's compliance decision. The result is enforcement that diverges from the legal posture the publisher chose, in either direction, without any error surfacing in the auction.

educate.base:
Prebid offers two GPP control modules for US privacy because there are two ways to encode the same choices. The 'gppControl_usnat' module reads the US national string, section 7 of the Global Privacy Platform (GPP). The 'gppControl_usstates' module reads the individual state strings, sections 8 through 12, and translates them into an equivalent national string so it can apply the same activity restrictions. The two modules are alternatives that match the encoding the publisher's consent management platform (CMP) sends, and the choice between national and state encoding is a legal decision the publisher makes with counsel.

Prebid's own guidance leans toward the national string. The documentation states that national string support is mature and less error prone while new state strings are still being released, so it encourages publishers to consider, with their legal counsel, using the national string over the state strings. A publisher who has decided on national handling but whose CMP emits state sections, or the reverse, has a gap between the compliance decision and what the page enforces.

The mismatch is quiet because Prebid does not judge the signal, it acts on it. The control module applies restrictions based on whatever sections the GPP string presents for the visitor. If the CMP applies the wrong sections for a visitor's state, or sends a national string to a control module built for state strings, the restrictions still apply but they reflect the signal as sent rather than the posture the publisher meant to enforce. The framework behaves correctly on an incorrect input.

A few signs point to a signal that does not match the intended jurisdiction logic:
  • The loaded control module does not match the encoding the CMP sends, for example 'gppControl_usnat' is loaded while the CMP emits state sections, or the reverse
  • The 'applicableSections' the CMP reports for a test visitor do not match the sections the publisher's compliance plan expects for that visitor's state
  • The publisher cannot state whether the implementation is built around the national string or the state strings, which suggests the choice was inherited rather than decided
You may want to confirm that the GPP signal the page sends matches the jurisdiction logic the publisher decided on with counsel. One approach is to simulate visitors from a few states and read the 'applicableSections' and the loaded control module to see whether they agree with the intended approach.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site with a national audience, visitors arrive from many states, so the encoding choice between the national string and the state strings affects a wide spread of traffic at once. You may want to test the signal against visitors simulated from several states rather than a single one because a mismatch may surface only for the states whose sections the CMP handles differently.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, the privacy posture interacts with the customer data the business already holds, so the GPP encoding should agree with how the rest of the business treats state privacy. You may want to confirm the Prebid control module matches the same national-versus-state decision the business applies elsewhere rather than enforcing a different one in the ad stack.

learn_more.source_title: GPP Control Module - US state strings

investigate.intro: Identify which GPP control module is loaded, then compare the sections the consent management platform reports for test visitors against the jurisdiction logic the publisher intended.

browser_console.label: Browser console and Prebid debug
steps: Open the page in developer tools and run 'pbjs.installedModules' to see whether 'gppControl_usnat' or 'gppControl_usstates' is loaded.
Run 'pbjs.getConfig('consentManagement')' and confirm the 'gpp' object's 'cmpApi' setting and any static 'consentData' that names a 'sectionList' or 'applicableSections'.
Use the CMP's own debug or geo-simulation feature to present as a visitor from a few different states, and record the 'applicableSections' and GPP string the CMP returns for each.

Compare the loaded control module and the reported sections against the publisher's documented national-versus-state decision, and note any case where the encoding the page sends differs from the intended approach.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records which GPP control module is loaded, the 'applicableSections' the CMP reports for test visitors from a few states, and the publisher's intended national-versus-state approach. A setup where the loaded module matches the encoding the CMP sends and the sections agree with the documented jurisdiction decision passes. A setup where the module and the encoding disagree, or where the reported sections do not match the publisher's intended approach for a given state, is a fail recorded with the states and sections where the signal and the intent diverge.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the loaded control module, the reported 'applicableSections' for test states, and the intended jurisdiction approach above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the Global Privacy Platform (GPP) signal {{site_name}} sends does not match the jurisdiction logic the implementation intends, so Prebid enforces a signal that diverges from the publisher's compliance decision. Prebid offers a national control module, 'gppControl_usnat', and a state control module, 'gppControl_usstates', and each matches a different encoding the consent management platform can send, so the loaded module and the sections the platform reports have to agree with the national-versus-state approach the publisher chose with counsel. {{voice_recommend}} simulating visitors from several states, reading the loaded control module and the reported applicable sections for each, and aligning the consent management platform output with the control module so the signal the page sends reflects the intended jurisdiction logic. Prebid's own guidance favors the national string as the more mature and less error-prone option, which is worth weighing with legal counsel.
